events.text: Patient is a 72-year-old Caucasian male with a history of chronic lymphocytic leukemia in remission, presenting with a thick pigmented nodule on his anterior chest.

At age 72, patient was diagnosed with nodular melanoma (ICD-O morphology 8721/3) of the skin of the trunk (ICD-O topography C44.5), staged as Stage IV (T4bN1aM1b) with synchronous pulmonary metastases at presentation.

At age 72, excisional biopsy of the anterior chest wall lesion revealed nodular melanoma with Breslow thickness of 7.8 mm, Clark level V, ulceration present, mitotic rate 15/mm2, extensive lymphovascular invasion, microsatellites present, and perineural invasion present.
At age 72, wide local excision with 2 cm margins of the anterior chest melanoma was performed for local disease control with advancement flap closure.
At age 72, sentinel lymph node biopsy of the left axilla showed 1 of 2 sentinel nodes positive for macrometastatic melanoma deposit measuring 8.2 mm.
At age 72, sentinel node pathology confirmed macrometastatic melanoma in 1 of 2 nodes (8.2 mm deposit) with extracapsular extension, S100 and SOX10 positive, HMB-45 positive.
At age 72, staging CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis revealed multiple bilateral pulmonary metastases (largest 3.1 cm right upper lobe), mediastinal lymphadenopathy, and no hepatic or osseous metastases.
At age 72, MRI brain with gadolinium showed a single 2.2 cm enhancing lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere consistent with brain metastasis.

At age 72, next-generation sequencing of the primary melanoma identified BRAF V600E mutation (allele frequency 60%), NRAS wild-type, TERT promoter mutation (C228T), TP53 Y220C mutation, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PIK3CA E545K mutation, and tumor mutational burden of 22 mutations per megabase.
At age 72, serum LDH was markedly elevated at 520 U/L, ECOG performance status was 1, and disease was classified as Stage IV M1d given brain metastasis.
At age 72, stereotactic radiosurgery was delivered to the right cerebellar metastasis at a dose of 21 Gy in 1 fraction.
At age 72, combination dabrafenib 150 mg twice daily plus trametinib 2 mg daily was initiated for BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV melanoma with brain metastasis given need for rapid disease control.

At age 72, patient developed grade 2 pyrexia (temperature 39.5 C) and grade 2 fatigue within the first week of dabrafenib/trametinib, managed with dose interruption of dabrafenib for 3 days and restart at 100 mg twice daily.
At age 72, patient developed grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 stools/day above baseline) attributed to trametinib, managed with loperamide.
At age 72, restaging CT at 8 weeks showed partial response with 45% reduction in pulmonary metastases and decreased mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
At age 72, MRI brain at 8 weeks post-SRS showed decreased enhancement of the cerebellar lesion with surrounding radiation effect and no new intracranial disease.
At age 72, LDH decreased to 345 U/L reflecting partial biochemical response to dabrafenib/trametinib therapy.
At age 73, restaging CT at 6 months showed continued partial response in pulmonary metastases but emergence of a new 1.8 cm hepatic lesion concerning for progressive disease.
At age 73, LDH rose to 412 U/L suggesting progressive disease, and the decision was made to transition to immunotherapy.
At age 73, dabrafenib/trametinib was discontinued and nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks was initiated as second-line therapy.
At age 73, restaging CT at 12 weeks on nivolumab showed stable pulmonary disease and slight increase in hepatic lesion suggesting pseudo-progression versus true progression.
At age 73, clinical decision was made to continue nivolumab given overall stable disease burden and patient maintained ECOG performance status of 1 with LDH stable at 398 U/L.

scenario_blurb: Stage III/IV melanoma with BRAF V600E mutation. Treated with combination BRAF/MEK inhibitors (dabrafenib/trametinib) and/or checkpoint immunotherapy (nivolumab plus ipilimumab, pembrolizumab). Some with brain metastases. Include LDH levels.
